text.title: TAM/SAM/SOM Definitions and Calculation Methods
text.summary: Definitions, formulas, and three calculation methods (top-down, bottom-up, competitor-based) with worked examples.

rule.statement: Define TAM, SAM, and SOM explicitly in the prompt on every agent run. Never assume the agent carries definitions across sessions.
rule.rationale: LLMs use these terms interchangeably based on training data. Without explicit definitions, outputs will conflate them and produce inconsistent estimates that cannot be compared across methods.

section.title: TAM: Total Addressable Market
section.p: Total revenue if the product captured 100% of the market. Formula: total potential customers x average revenue per customer. Use case: show the ceiling; not realistic but shows opportunity size.

example.code: All small businesses globally: 400M Average software spend: $1,000/year TAM = 400M x $1,000 = $400B
example.note: TAM is the ceiling, not the target. Investors know this; never present TAM as achievable.

section.title: SAM: Serviceable Available Market
section.p: Portion of TAM that can actually be served given constraints (geography, language, industry, company size, tech stack). Formula: TAM x segment percentage.

example.code: TAM: $400B small business software Constraints: US-based, e-commerce, 1-50 employees SAM = $400B x 2% = $8B
example.note: Each constraint must be justified with data or a sourced estimate. Stacking four unsourced percentages creates compounding error.

section.title: SOM: Serviceable Obtainable Market
section.p: Realistic market share capturable in 3-5 years. Formula: SAM x realistic market share. Realistic share: Year 1 = 0.1-0.5%, Year 3 = 1-3%, Year 5 = 3-10%.

example.code: SAM: $8B Year 3 target: 0.5% share SOM = $8B x 0.5% = $40M Express as: 4,000 customers x $10,000/year = $40M
example.note: Always express SOM as customer count first, dollars second. "4,000 customers" is testable; "$40M" is not.

rule.statement: Run all three methods independently before triangulation. Never run bottom-up after reviewing top-down results — anchoring bias inflates the bottom-up estimate.
rule.rationale: If the agent sees a $10B top-down TAM before computing the bottom-up, it will unconsciously reverse-engineer to produce a consistent number. Independence requires sequential blind computation.

section.title: Method 1: Top-Down
section.p: Start with industry reports, narrow down: Industry Size → Segment → Geography → SOM. Pros: uses established data. Cons: can be disconnected from reality if segment assumptions are unsourced.

section.title: Method 2: Bottom-Up
section.p: Start with customers, multiply up: # potential customers x conversion rate x price = revenue. Pros: grounded in reality. Cons: may underestimate opportunity; requires accurate customer counts.

rule.statement: For B2B markets, use government establishment data (US Census BDS, Eurostat) as the top-down customer count anchor — it is free and primary.
rule.rationale: LinkedIn Sales Navigator counts are inaccessible for pipelines; Statista customer counts are paywalled. Government data is free, primary, and reliable for establishment counts by NAICS/NACE code.

section.title: Method 3: Competitor-Based
section.p: Use competitor revenues to estimate total market: sum named competitor revenues + extrapolate long tail (typically 1.5x of named players). Sources: Crunchbase funding, SimilarWeb traffic estimates, LinkedIn employee count x $200K (B2B SaaS only, 20+ employees).

antipattern.description: LinkedIn employee count x $200K rule used for companies under 20 people or for services businesses.
antipattern.reason: The rule undercounts services and overcounts seed-stage SaaS. It is only valid for growth-stage B2B SaaS companies where sales-to-headcount ratios are established.
